does: This paper presents a method for training Transformers that are interpretable by design rather than requiring post-hoc reverse-engineering. The authors develop a modified Transformer architecture that can be trained with gradient descent and then automatically converted into discrete, human-readable programs expressed in RASP, a domain-specific language compilable to Transformer weights. They validate the approach by learning Transformer Programs for diverse tasks: in-context learning, algorithmic problems like sorting and Dyck language recognition, and NLP tasks including named entity recognition and text classification. The key contribution is bridging the gap between interpretability and trainability by creating models whose learned weights can be directly translated into explicit algorithmic descriptions.
connects: This work builds directly on RASP (Weiss et al., 2021), which demonstrated that Transformers can be compiled from discrete programs, though that direction was one-way: human programs to weights. The paper responds to the mechanistic interpretability literature, which has focused on post-hoc analysis through weight inspection and activation examination of trained models, as exemplified by work on circuit discovery and attention head probing. Rather than attempting to reverse-engineer arbitrary trained models through careful analysis, this approach inverts the compilation direction, combining ideas from programming language design with machine learning to make interpretability a first-class training objective alongside task performance.
enables: This framework enables several future directions in interpretable machine learning. It opens the possibility of systematically studying what algorithms Transformers learn for various tasks and comparing learned solutions to human-designed ones. The approach could facilitate verification and validation of neural networks by translating learned behaviors into auditable code. It points toward a new paradigm where interpretability constraints are built into architecture and training rather than applied after learning, potentially enabling safer and more trustworthy AI systems. The method could also extend to other architectures and scaling challenges, and might support interactive refinement where humans and models collaborate to improve both performance and interpretability.
